draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to warm, conversational language that feels like a friend giving advice rather than corporate software speaking at them
facade.label: Casual chatty tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational, first-person voice; use contractions; make copy feel like peer-to-peer guidance
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward polished, neutral, business-standard voice; remove personality markers; prioritize clarity over warmth

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer chronological narrative of client relationships over tabular contact lists
facade.label: Client timeline view
facade.acceptImplies: Build timeline cards showing client history, milestones, and touchpoints as flowing story rather than rows in a database table
facade.rejectImplies: Lean toward dense data tables, quick-scan spreadsheet layouts, and metrics-first presentation of client information

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to warm, conversational language that feels like a friend giving advice rather than corporate software speaking at them
evidence.implication: Lean into conversational, first-person voice; use contractions; make copy feel like peer-to-peer guidance

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to soft, inviting interface colors over stark white or cool tones, suggesting they value approachability in their tools
facade.label: Warm beige cards
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use warm, earthy color palettes (beiges, warm grays, soft oranges) to feel personal and less corporate
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM might need cooler or higher-contrast color schemes (whites, grays, blues) to feel modern, efficient, and business-focused

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need to see everything at once — projects, invoices, messages — even if it feels busy and overwhelming.
facade.axisTargeted: Organic (information-dense, natural accumulation) vs Digital (curated, sparse, filtered)
facade.label: Cluttered dashboard
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward information-rich interfaces where all key data is visible simultaneously; may tolerate visual noise for completeness.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers calm, focused views with hidden complexity; trusts progressive disclosure and clean hierarchy over comprehensive at-a-glance visibility.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer fluid, animated state changes (contacts appearing, records sliding in, notifications gliding) over instant jumps, because motion creates sense of responsiveness and care in a tool they use dozens of times daily.
facade.axisTargeted: Animated motion vs instant state changes
facade.label: Smooth transitions
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize easing curves, slide-ins, fade-ins, and choreographed transitions throughout the CRM — especially in list updates, modal opens, and data loads. Motion is a core feature, not decoration.
facade.rejectImplies: Motion is overhead. User wants snappy, instant UI with no delay — pure function. Transitions would feel slow. Build toward zero-latency feedback instead.

facade.hypothesis: For a freelancer CRM, a persistent left sidebar housing client navigation (list view) paired with a right content area showing client details as expandable cards would reduce cognitive load and enable quick scanning of all clients while maintaining focused detail views.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation containment and information density—specifically whether navigation lives persistently (sidebar) or transiently (tabs/modal), and whether client records display as dense lists or spacious cards
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward persistent, accessible navigation with generous whitespace around individual client records; likely values quick context-switching and readability over maximum data visibility per viewport
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers either (a) horizontal tab-based navigation, or (b) denser list layouts, suggesting they want flatter hierarchy and less screen real estate devoted to navigation chrome, or value compact scanning of multiple records simultaneously

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business-like assistant or a supportive collaborator
Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
Whether user prefers data-heavy dashboards with metrics/charts vs. simple lists and summaries—reveals how much visibility into business health they need
Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals preference for quick task completion vs. thoughtful pacing

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business-like assistant or a supportive collaborator
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers data-heavy dashboards with metrics/charts vs. simple lists and summaries—reveals how much visibility into business health they need
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals preference for quick task completion vs. thoughtful pacing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers perceive all-caps section headers as authoritative and corporate, potentially creating distance; sentence case or title case maintains warmth while preserving scanability
facade.label: All-caps headers
facade.acceptImplies: Use sentence case or title case for headers to preserve the conversational, peer-to-peer tone already accepted; avoid typography that signals corporate distance
facade.rejectImplies: All-caps headers are acceptable in this context; the friendly tone doesn't require softening typography; hierarchy can be visual without relying on case sensitivity
